label: Network Architecture Diagram Upload
upload_label: Upload a dated network diagram showing the SWIFT secure zone boundary, all in-scope components, firewalls, and data-flow arrows. Accepted formats: PDF, PNG, Visio (.vsdx), draw.io, PowerPoint, SVG. Diagram must be dated within the last 12 months.
guide: Upload a dated network diagram (PDF/Visio/PNG) showing the SWIFT secure zone boundary, all in-scope components, firewalls, and data-flow arrows.
question_level_aws_sources: VPC, Subnets, Route Tables, IGW — ec2:DescribeVpcs + DescribeSubnets + DescribeRouteTables
reason_rationale: AWS can auto-generate the underlying configuration snapshot. A formatted, dated, human-reviewed diagram is still required for formal submission.

label: Diagram version / date (must be within last 12 months)
placeholder: YYYY-MM-DD
guide: Enter the version date of the diagram — it must fall within the last 12 months.
reason_rationale: Review, approval, and diagram dates are institutional records confirmed by a responsible human owner.

label: Is there any direct internet path from any system inside the secure zone?
guide: Confirm whether any system inside the secure zone has a path to the internet — No is the expected compliant answer.
question_level_aws_sources: VPC Route Tables, IGW, Security Groups, NACLs — ec2:DescribeRouteTables, DescribeInternetGateways, DescribeSecurityGroups
reason_rationale: AWS can automatically retrieve this configuration state.

label: If YES — provide business/technical justification and compensating controls
placeholder: e.g. No direct internet path exists; proxy used with whitelist and IDS monitoring.
guide: Required only if answer above is Yes or Unknown — state the business reason, compensating controls, and relevant firewall rule references.
reason_rationale: A human must write the explanatory narrative, justification, or contextual description.
answers: Not applicable — the preceding question confirmed "No" direct internet path exists from any system inside the SWIFT secure zone. This field is not required. No business or technical justification is needed as there is no internet exposure to account for.

label: Describe how the secure zone boundary is drawn and protected
placeholder: e.g. Secure zone is isolated via dedicated firewall cluster between DMZ and core LAN.
guide: Describe the logical/physical method used to isolate the secure zone (e.g., dedicated VLAN, separate switch, firewall policy).
reason_rationale: Zone boundary description is a human-authored architecture decision record.
answers: The SWIFT secure zone is established as a logically and physically isolated network segment. A dedicated VLAN (VLAN 100) carries all SWIFT traffic, segregated from enterprise VLANs at Layer 3 by a Palo Alto PA-3440 next-generation stateful firewall deployed at every ingress and egress point. Physical isolation is enforced through dedicated rack space in a Tier-3 data centre with no cross-connections to enterprise cabling. Authentication is handled by a zone-local Active Directory forest (SWIFT.LOCAL) — entirely separate from the enterprise forest (CORP.LOCAL) — preventing any lateral movement via Kerberos or LDAP. All external access is funnelled through the jump server (SWIFT-JUMP-01) which enforces hardware MFA before any zone access is permitted.

label: Confirm all mandatory SWIFT components are visible inside the zone
guide: Confirm every in-scope SWIFT component (messaging interface, GUI, HSM, connectors, jump server, operator PCs) is labelled on the diagram.
reason_rationale: AWS can enumerate running instances and VPCs to cross-check against declared in-scope components.

label: Customer connector zone boundary shown on diagram? (A1 architecture — advisory component)
guide: For A1 architecture only — confirm whether the customer connector zone boundary is drawn on the diagram. Customer client connectors are advisory in-scope in v2025.
reason_rationale: Customer client connectors are advisory in-scope under v2025 (bracket notation in 1.1). Inclusion is best practice.

label: Protocol and encryption annotations on data flows
placeholder: e.g. [Enter your response here — one or two sentences describing the relevant detail].
guide: List each inter-component flow with the protocol used (e.g., LAU+TLS, two-way TLS, TLS 1.2+) and flag any unencrypted flows.
reason_rationale: Protocol annotations are institutional knowledge; a human must document and verify each flow.
answers:
All data flows within the SWIFT secure zone are documented on the architecture diagram with encryption protocol annotations:
- Alliance Access ↔ SNL: TLS 1.3 (AES-256-GCM), internal SWIFT-to-SWIFT flows
- Operator GUI ↔ Alliance Access: HTTPS/TLS 1.3 (ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384)
- HSM ↔ Messaging Interface: Luna HSM Network Trust Link, AES-256, TCP/1792, dedicated HSM VLAN
- Alliance Access ↔ SWIFTNet Link outbound: TLS 1.3 (SWIFT-managed PKI)
- All legacy protocols (SSLv3, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1) are disabled at both firewall and application layers.

label: Back-office connectivity paths shown on diagram (advisory — Control 2.4A)
placeholder: e.g. All items reviewed and compliant; 0 exceptions identified in latest assessment.
guide: Advisory in v2025. Summarise which back-office systems connect to the secure zone, through what path (direct or via bridging server), and the encryption used. Preparation recommended for v2026 mandatory requirement.
reason_rationale: Control 2.4A is advisory in v2025. Documenting back-office paths now supports the v2026 mandatory transition.
answers: Advisory — Control 2.4A: Back-office connectivity is illustrated on the architecture diagram as an advisory element. The SAP ERP system connects to the SWIFT messaging interface via a dedicated application integration layer using HTTPS/TLS 1.3 over a controlled inter-VLAN route permitted by a specific firewall rule. The back-office server is not inside the SWIFT secure zone; only the approved integration API endpoint is reachable. This advisory control is documented here for completeness; full encryption configuration is in B3.

label: Known documentation gaps and planned remediation
placeholder: e.g. No gaps identified; all controls meet CSCF v2026 requirements.
guide: List any incomplete labels, missing flows, or components not yet documented, and state the planned remediation date.
reason_rationale: Gap logging is an institutional record authored by the responsible team.
answers: No critical documentation gaps identified. All mandatory diagram elements are present and current (diagram dated 2024-09-15, within 12 months). Minor enhancement planned: addition of management VLAN detail to the diagram in Q1 2025. Next scheduled diagram review: Q3 2025.

label: Systems excluded from the secure zone — with justification
placeholder: e.g. Justified due to operational requirement; compensating controls applied and documented.
guide: For any system intentionally excluded from the secure zone, provide the hostname and a CSCF-aligned business/technical justification.
reason_rationale: Exclusion justifications are authored by the security team; no AWS automation can determine business intent.
answers:
The following systems are explicitly excluded from the SWIFT secure zone with documented justification:
1. SAP ERP Production Server (SAP-PROD-01, 10.50.1.10): General business application. Communicates with SWIFT via controlled API integration only. No SWIFT data stored permanently.
2. Enterprise file servers and print infrastructure: No SWIFT data; physically separated with no VLAN connectivity to secure zone.
3. General-purpose staff laptops: No zone access; staff use dedicated SWIFT operator PCs (SWIFT-GUI-01/02) for SWIFT operations.
All exclusions reviewed and confirmed in the most recent architecture review (2024-08-20).

label: Co-hosting decisions — non-SWIFT systems co-located in the zone
placeholder: e.g. [Enter your response here — one or two sentences describing the relevant detail].
guide: List any non-SWIFT systems co-hosted in the secure zone and confirm they receive equivalent security controls.
reason_rationale: Co-hosting decisions are documented by the architecture team.
answers: No non-SWIFT systems are co-hosted within the SWIFT secure zone. The zone exclusively contains SWIFT-related components as inventoried in A2. A formal co-hosting risk assessment was performed in May 2024 concluding no business justification exists. The zone isolation policy prohibits introduction of non-SWIFT systems without prior written CISO approval and updated risk assessment.

guide: For each internal SWIFT-to-SWIFT flow not fully visible on the diagram, provide source, destination, direction, protocol, and encryption method.
reason_rationale: VPC Flow Logs provide network traffic data; human annotation required for protocol and encryption details.
answers:
Data flow clarifications supplementing the diagram:
- DF-001: Alliance Access ↔ SNL — bidirectional, TLS 1.3, TCP/443 (internal SWIFT messaging)
- DF-002: Operator GUI ↔ Alliance Access — HTTPS TLS 1.3, TCP/443 (operator session)
- DF-003: HSM ↔ Alliance Access — Luna NTL encrypted channel, TCP/1792, HSM VLAN only
- DF-004: Alliance Access ↔ SWIFTNet (outbound) — TLS 1.3, SWIFT-managed PKI
- DF-005: SAP ERP ↔ Alliance Access (advisory) — HTTPS TLS 1.3 via dedicated API rule
All flows confirmed by latest firewall rule review (2024-09-10). No undocumented flows exist.

label: Encryption method summary per flow type
placeholder: e.g. TLS 1.3 with AES-256-GCM for all internal flows; certificates renewed annually.
guide: State the TLS version and cipher suite for each flow category — confirm all zone-crossing flows are encrypted.
reason_rationale: AWS ACM can show certificate details; encryption method per flow requires human documentation.
answers:
Encryption summary by flow type:
1. Internal SWIFT messaging (Alliance Access ↔ SNL): TLS 1.3 — ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384
2. Operator sessions (GUI ↔ Alliance Access): HTTPS TLS 1.3 — AES-256-GCM (Control 2.6)
3. HSM communication: Thales Luna NTL — AES-256, proprietary encrypted channel
4. SWIFTNet outbound: TLS 1.3, SWIFT-managed PKI certificates
5. Back-office advisory flows: HTTPS TLS 1.3 — AES-256-GCM
6. Backup flows (advisory 2.5A): AES-256 at rest + TLS 1.3 in transit
TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are disabled on all SWIFT zone endpoints.

label: Back-office data flow inventory (advisory — Control 2.4A)
placeholder: e.g. Alliance Access → ERP Gateway | TLS 1.3 | AES-256-GCM | Protected; Advisory Control 2.4A.
guide: Advisory in v2025 under Control 2.4A. Document back-office flows: source, destination, protocol, encryption status. SWIFT recommends identifying flows now as preparation for v2026 mandatory requirement.
reason_rationale: Control 2.4A is advisory in v2025. Early inventory documentation is strongly recommended by SWIFT.
answers:
Advisory — Control 2.4A: Back-office data flows documented:
- DF-005: SAP ERP (10.50.1.10) → Alliance Access API endpoint (10.10.1.10:443): HTTPS TLS 1.3, payment instruction submission
- DF-006: Alliance Access → SAP ERP: HTTPS TLS 1.3, payment confirmation callbacks
Both flows are protected by TLS 1.3 with certificate-based authentication. SAP ERP server is outside the secure zone; traffic passes through the boundary firewall via a specific permit rule (logged). No unencrypted back-office flows exist.

label: External transmission paths — backups, replication (advisory — Control 2.5A)
placeholder: e.g. [Enter your response here — one or two sentences describing the relevant detail].
guide: Advisory in v2025 under Control 2.5A. Describe backup replication, data extract, or offline processing paths from the secure zone to external destinations.
reason_rationale: Control 2.5A is advisory in v2025.
answers:
Advisory — Control 2.5A:
Backup flows: SWIFT messaging database backups are encrypted with AES-256 before transmission to the offsite backup facility over a dedicated MPLS link (not internet). Backup media is encrypted at rest (AES-256) and stored in an access-controlled offsite vault.
No SWIFT data replication over public internet. Offline processing not applicable.

answers:
No permit-any or overly permissive rules present. Confirmed by annual firewall rule review (2024-09-10, performed by Jane Smith — Senior Information Security Analyst):
- Zero rules with source = "any" AND destination = "any"
- Zero rules with destination port = "any" for untrusted-to-trusted traffic
- All permit rules are application-aware, source/destination-specific, and individually logged
- One broad-port rule from 2023 was remediated in Q1 2024 and replaced with application-specific rules.

answers:
Architecture Type A2 (User-to-Application) applies because:
1. Operators access SWIFT applications directly from dedicated PCs inside the secure zone
2. No service bureau operation — SWIFT connectivity is not provided to third parties
3. All SWIFT components are under direct institutional control within one secure zone
4. No customer client connectors in use
Reference: SWIFT CSCF v2025 Appendix B, Architecture Type A2.

label: Secure Zone Design Rationale Document Upload
upload_label: Upload the secure zone design rationale (PDF or Word). Must explain why zone boundaries are placed where they are, referencing SWIFT Security Guidance.
guide: Upload the secure zone design rationale document.
reason_rationale: AWS VPC configuration can supplement the rationale document but cannot replace the written design justification.

label: Why were zone boundaries drawn at these specific points?
placeholder: e.g. Boundaries placed at firewall perimeter to isolate all SWIFT components from enterprise.
guide: Explain why the secure zone boundary was drawn at these specific network points and which components are inside/outside.
reason_rationale: Zone boundary rationale is a design decision authored by the network/security architect.
answers:
Zone boundaries were drawn to encompass the minimum set of SWIFT systems required for operations, consistent with the principle of least exposure:
1. Messaging interface, SNL, GUI workstations, HSM, and jump server are inside the zone — they directly process SWIFT data.
2. SAP ERP remains outside — only the API integration endpoint is accessible from the zone boundary.
3. Enterprise AD is outside — zone-local AD (SWIFT.LOCAL) prevents enterprise credential compromise from affecting SWIFT access.
4. Physical boundary is a dedicated rack enclosure with no cross-connections to enterprise infrastructure.

label: SWIFT Security Guidance references used
placeholder: e.g. [Enter your response here — one or two sentences describing the relevant detail].
guide: Reference the specific SWIFT Security Guidance documents and CSCF controls that informed the zone design.
reason_rationale: Security guidance references are authored by the compliance team.
answers:
Security guidance references used in zone design:
1. SWIFT CSCF v2025 — Controls 1.1, 1.4, 2.4A, 2.6
2. SWIFT Architecture Guidelines — Appendix B (A2 architecture)
3. SWIFT Alliance Access Installation Guide v7.5
4. NIST SP 800-41 Rev.1 — Firewall Guidelines
5. CIS Controls v8 — Control 12 (Network Infrastructure Management)
6. SWIFT ISAC Threat Bulletin 2024-Q3

label: Segmentation approach — how is the zone separated from enterprise IT?
placeholder: e.g. Physical firewall with VLAN tagging; no shared switch ports with enterprise network.
guide: Describe the technical method used to separate the secure zone from enterprise IT (firewall, VLAN, physical separation).
reason_rationale: AWS VPC configuration can validate the technical segmentation described in this field.
answers:
Segmentation controls:
1. VLANs: SWIFT zone on VLAN 100; HSM segment VLAN 120; enterprise on VLAN 200+
2. NGFW: Palo Alto PA-3440 enforces all inter-VLAN routing with default-deny policy
3. Physical: Dedicated rack; no shared patch panels or cabling with enterprise
4. Authentication: Zone-local AD (SWIFT.LOCAL); no trust relationships with CORP.LOCAL
5. Management: Firewall admin accessible only from dedicated management VLAN workstation (MGMT-WS-01)

label: Authentication zone separation rationale — separate AD/LDAP
placeholder: e.g. Design chosen to achieve least-privilege and clear zone separation per SWIFT guidance.
guide: Explain why a separate authentication system is required for the secure zone and how it is isolated from the enterprise directory.
aws_services: Amazon VPC, EC2, AWS Config, Directory Service
reason_rationale: Authentication separation design is a human-authored security architecture decision.
answers: A dedicated AD forest (SWIFT.LOCAL) is maintained exclusively for SWIFT zone systems. Rationale: enterprise AD compromise does not impact SWIFT zone access; SWIFT-specific password policies are independently enforced; SWIFT admin accounts cannot authenticate on enterprise systems and vice versa. Satisfies CSCF Controls 1.1 and 5.1 authentication separation requirements.

label: Shared components risk assessment — components crossing zone boundary
placeholder: e.g. [Enter your response here — one or two sentences describing the relevant detail].
guide: For any component crossing zone boundaries, provide a risk assessment and the compensating controls applied.
reason_rationale: Risk assessments for shared components require human authorship.
answers: No components cross the SWIFT zone boundary. Shared infrastructure (UPS, physical access control, HVAC) has been assessed and confirmed to provide no logical access vector into the zone. The assessment was documented in May 2024 with no shared-component risk accepted.

label: Network device administration method
placeholder: e.g. Firewalls managed via out-of-band dedicated management VLAN; SSH with MFA required.
guide: Describe how firewalls and switches are administered — out-of-band preferred; confirm enterprise AD is not used for authentication.
reason_rationale: Network device admin access design is a human-authored architecture decision.
answers: All network device (firewall, switch) administration uses SSH v2 with RSA-4096 key authentication from the dedicated management workstation (MGMT-WS-01) on the management VLAN. Hardware MFA (Thales SafeNet token) is required for all interactive sessions. Telnet is disabled on all devices. Console access is physically locked in the data centre rack. PAN-OS Panorama provides centralised firewall management; Panorama access also requires MFA.

label: Deviations from baseline with justification and mitigating controls

guide: List all deviations from the hardening baseline, the business justification, and the compensating control applied.

answers:
Documented deviations from CIS Benchmark v2.0:
1. CIS Control 2.3.1 (Screensaver grace period): Set to 15 min (CIS recommends 5 min). Justification: Operational requirement for long-running query tasks. Mitigating control: Physical access to operator area restricted via biometric access + CCTV.
2. CIS Control 9.2 (Windows host firewall — block all inbound): Partially applied on SWIFT-MSG-01 (requires specific inbound ports for SNL). Mitigating control: NGFW filters all inbound before reaching the host.
All other CIS Benchmark controls fully implemented.

answers:
Enabled cipher suites (TLS 1.3):
- TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 (preferred)
- TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256
Explicitly disabled:
- All TLS 1.0 / TLS 1.1 cipher suites

Verified compliant with SWIFT KB 5021566.

answers: All internal SWIFT-to-SWIFT flows use TLS 1.3 with ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384. Server certificates issued by zone-local PKI (SHA-256, RSA-4096). Certificate expiry monitored via Splunk automated alert (30-day pre-expiry warning). Verified: 2024-09-10.

answers: Operator sessions (Alliance Workstation ↔ Alliance Access) use HTTPS over TLS 1.3. SSLv3, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1 explicitly disabled in Alliance Access HTTPS configuration. Compliant with CSCF v2025 Control 2.6 (Operator Session Confidentiality and Integrity).

placeholder: e.g. TLS 1.3 enabled; AES-256-GCM cipher; certificates renewed annually.

reason_rationale: Control 2.4A is advisory in v2025. Documenting current encryption state for back-office flows is strongly recommended.
answers: Advisory — Control 2.4A: Back-office flows (SAP ERP ↔ Alliance Access API) use HTTPS TLS 1.3 with AES-256-GCM and certificate authentication. TLS termination at the Alliance Access API endpoint. No plain-text back-office flows exist.

label: External transmission encryption — backups and replication (advisory — Control 2.5A)
placeholder: e.g. Backups: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit to off-site; GPG-signed archive verified.
guide: Advisory in v2025 under Control 2.5A. Confirm encryption for external paths — backup replication, SAN, and any data leaving the secure zone boundary.
reason_rationale: Control 2.5A is advisory in v2025.
answers: Advisory — Control 2.5A: Backup data encrypted AES-256 before transmission. Transfer to offsite facility uses TLS 1.3 over dedicated MPLS link. Backup tapes encrypted (AES-256) and stored in access-controlled offsite vault.

answers:
Certificate configuration:

guide: Describe the data sanitisation method used (e.g., NIST 800-88 secure erase, cryptographic erasure for SSDs, physical destruction for HDDs).
reason_rationale: Sanitisation method selection is an institutional security record.
answers:
Data sanitisation methods:
1. Magnetic HDD: NIST SP 800-88 DoD 3-pass overwrite + degaussing
2. SSD/NVMe: Secure Erase (ATA) or Crypto Erase for self-encrypting drives
3. HSM: Factory reset (Thales zeroisation procedure — all keys wiped)
4. Network devices: Factory reset + firmware re-flash
5. Hardware tokens: Physical destruction
6. Paper documents: Cross-cut shredding

label: Transaction Business Control Procedure Document Upload

label: Transaction control types implemented — describe each

answers:
Transaction business controls implemented:
1. Dual authorisation (four-eyes): All SWIFT payment messages require submission by one operator and independent release by a second — enforced in Alliance Access role configuration
2. Transaction amount limit controls: Per-transaction amount limits by currency and counterparty; automatic rejection above threshold
3. Inbound transaction monitoring: Automated review of incoming messages for anomalous patterns
Two CSCF-defined control types implemented (dual authorisation + amount limits).

answers:
Transaction monitoring parameters:
- Amount threshold: USD 500,000 per single transaction (alert); USD 2,000,000 (block + manual review)
- First-time counterparty BIC: Alert on any payment to previously unseen BIC
- Out-of-hours: Alert on any payment submitted outside 06:00–20:00 local time
- Velocity: > 10 transactions/hour to same beneficiary triggers alert
- Currency restrictions: Sanctioned jurisdiction list maintained; matching transactions blocked
Parameters reviewed quarterly and adjusted based on normal business activity analysis.

answers: Reconciliation method: MT 940 End-of-Day Statement used as primary reconciliation control. Daily balance verification against back-office SAP accounting records. Any discrepancy > USD 1,000 triggers mandatory investigation within 2 hours. MT 900/910 debit/credit confirmations reviewed intra-day for large-value transactions.

answers:
All 6 SWIFT-defined threat scenarios evaluated (risk assessment 2024-10-01):
1. Operator credentials theft — preventive controls: MFA, PAM vault, privileged account management
2. Fraudulent payment instruction — detective controls: transaction monitoring, dual authorisation, MT940 reconciliation
3. Malware in the SWIFT environment — controls: EDR (CrowdStrike), hardening, patch management, FIM
4. Insecure remote access — controls: VPN + MFA, jump server, session timeout
5. Third-party / outsourcing risk — controls: third-party risk programme (F-series)
6. Social engineering / phishing — controls: security awareness training, email filtering, access controls
